lens.included_activities: US fine-arts schools in the lower-middle-market band providing recurring or repeat paid instruction in music, dance, drama, visual arts, crafts, or related nondegree arts disciplines to external households and other customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Degree-granting colleges, public K-12 schools, commercial and graphic-art career schools, captive programs, solo practices without transferable operations, pure performance or venue businesses, and instrument or art-supply retailers.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily household-paid recurring tuition or lesson fees, often monthly or term-based, supplemented by camps, recitals, competitions, materials, and private lessons; some schools also receive institutional contracts or grants.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for the broader four-digit Other Schools and Instruction industry, not 611610 or task exposure. I use its occupation shares and wages to construct a rough wage-weighted mix, then assign exposed-task shares by work type using observed teacher uses of AI. The broader industry includes tutoring, sports, and other instruction, likely overstating exposure for embodied fine-arts teaching, so the base is below a mechanical broader-industry estimate.
a.rationale: In the broader industry, educational occupations are 51.97% of employment, self-enrichment teachers alone are 30.23%, arts/media occupations are 13.30%, and office support is 9.87%. AI can draft lesson materials, communications, schedules, marketing, and routine records, but physical demonstration, nuanced critique, safeguarding, ensemble coordination, and live performance coaching remain difficult to substitute. A wage-weighted task decomposition therefore places roughly one-quarter of current work in the exposed opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A 611610-specific payroll export by occupation paired with time-use diaries from music, dance, drama, and visual-arts instructors and administrators, coded task by task for current automation, would materially change this range.
caveats: OEWS is available at NAICS 611600 rather than 611610 and excludes self-employed workers.
The teacher AI survey covers US public K-12 teachers, not private fine-arts schools.
Exposure is not the same as full job replacement; most exposed teaching tasks are partial-task assistance.

rho.bridge: A national public-K-12 teacher survey measures current AI use and reported time savings rather than five-year implementation of exposed tasks in private fine-arts schools. I translate 60% current use, reported six-hour weekly savings among weekly users, and uneven policy adoption into a five-year implementation share, with a downward adjustment for smaller schools' fragmented systems and child-privacy and quality controls. Public-school support and training may make the source overstate small-school implementation, while the five-year horizon may offset that bias.
rho.rationale: Current education adoption shows that the tools are usable: six in ten surveyed teachers used AI, weekly users reported about six hours saved, and users commonly saved time on worksheets, assessments, and administration. However, implementation across small arts schools must integrate enrollment, billing, scheduling, parent communications, curriculum, permissions, and human review; privacy and instructional-quality concerns prevent the entire exposed opportunity from being realized.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal benchmark from fine-arts school management platforms showing labor hours, AI feature activation, error rates, parent complaints, and headcount avoidance before and after adoption would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Reported time savings are self-reported and conditional on weekly tool use.
Public K-12 schools differ from private fine-arts schools in training, procurement, class structure, and governance.
The estimate assumes tools become integrated into workflow software within five years.

e.rationale: Most LMM-scale establishments classified as fine-arts schools sell repeat lessons or term-based instruction to external customers and therefore fit the service lens. The main exclusions are organizations whose economics are principally grants or performances, businesses centered on one irreplaceable owner-instructor, and mixed retail or venue operations without a separable recurring instruction business.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of 611610 firms in Data Axle or PitchBook, matched to websites, tax status, instructor count, revenue mix, and customer billing cadence, would reveal the actual eligible share.
caveats: The supplied LMM firm count is itself an estimate derived from size classes and a margin bridge.
Dance and fitness studio transactions demonstrate transferability for only part of 611610 and include fitness businesses outside the code.
Firm-level revenue mix and owner dependence are not available from public NAICS data.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year intentions across all US employer-business owners: 22% planned to sell or transfer ownership. I treat that as an upper anchor and reduce it for unrealized intentions, family gifts or reorganizations that may not convey qualifying control, and arts-school owner dependence. The source is likely to overstate completed qualifying transfers, while observed dance-studio sales show that an arm's-length market does exist.
s5.rationale: The broad employer-owner survey directly frames a five-year window, and more than half of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older. Yet intended transfers do not all close, and some fine-arts schools depend on the founder's teaching reputation or customer relationships. A completed qualifying-control probability in the mid-teens is therefore more defensible than using the 22% intention rate directly.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal counts from BizBuySell, Axial, DealStats, and state business-transfer records linked to a 611610 firm denominator, plus owner-age and succession interviews among LMM schools, would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup covers all industries and owner-reported plans, not completed deals.
BizBuySell data are a selected marketplace sample and combine dance with fitness studios.
Internal family transfers may or may not meet the qualifying-control definition.

q.rationale: Recurring tuition is usually a fixed customer price rather than cost-plus reimbursement, so near-term labor savings can remain with the operator. Over renewals, however, local competition, transparent lesson prices, instructor compensation pressure, scholarships, and customer expectations for smaller classes or richer programming share some benefit. The result is moderate rather than full retention of implemented gross benefit.
q.change_evidence: Five years of school-level cohort pricing, discounts, churn, instructor pay, class sizes, and contribution margins around documented workflow changes, supplemented by local competitor price panels, would materially narrow retention.
caveats: Public sources do not measure contractual pass-through or repricing after automation in this industry.
Private lessons, group classes, camps, and grant-supported programs have different pass-through dynamics.
The estimate excludes implementation difficulty and volume loss, which belong in other primitives.

d5.bridge: BLS projects self-enrichment-teacher employment, not constant-price demand for 611610 services, to rise 3.7% from 2024 to 2034. I use approximately half the ten-year change as a five-year demand anchor and widen substantially for productivity, demographic, price, and industry-composition differences. Employment may understate demand if productivity improves, but it may overstate private-school demand because the occupation spans other settings.
d5.rationale: The closest national forward indicator is broadly flat to modestly positive: self-enrichment-teacher employment is projected to increase from 417,500 to 433,000 over ten years. Adult arts learning is also an established activity in the NEA participation data. Aging youth demographics, discretionary household budgets, public-school arts provision, and digital alternatives create downside, while adult enrichment and hybrid offerings create upside; the base is therefore near flat in real, quality-adjusted quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A consistent five-year panel of 611610 paid enrollments, lesson-hours, retention, and real tuition from management-software vendors such as Jackrabbit or Sawyer, segmented by age and discipline, would materially change the range.
caveats: Occupational employment is not service demand and includes self-enrichment teachers outside fine-arts schools.
NEA adult participation measures engagement rather than purchases from private schools.
Demand is discretionary and locally exposed to income, child population, and public-program availability.

o.rationale: Most year-five paid quantity should still require an accountable school or instructor because customers buy live demonstration, physical correction, pacing, safeguarding, motivation, ensemble work, studio access, and performances, not just content. Recorded tutorials and AI feedback can replace part of beginner theory, practice planning, and asynchronous critique, but are more likely to complement than eliminate the operator for sustained dance, music, drama, and studio-art instruction.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level switching studies comparing renewal, attainment, and willingness to pay across human-led, hybrid, and AI-only arts instruction, plus five-year enrollment data from major online arts-learning platforms, would materially change this share.
caveats: No source directly measures operator displacement in private fine-arts instruction.
Operator requirement is lower for adult hobbyists and beginner theory than for children, ensembles, performance preparation, or movement disciplines.
Rapid improvement in multimodal real-time coaching would push the estimate downward.

narrative.executive_view: Fine-arts schools combine recurring tuition with a labor-heavy service, but only a minority of the work is plausibly substitutable: the core product is accountable human instruction and performance development. The nearer opportunity is to avoid administrative hiring and compress lesson preparation, marketing, enrollment, billing, scheduling, and routine communications. Firm transfers occur, though founder dependence and the small number of LMM targets constrain availability. Real demand appears broadly stable rather than structurally high-growth.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft curricula, lesson variants, practice plans, parent emails, marketing content, schedules, recital materials, and first-pass records; multimodal tools can also support asynchronous practice feedback. Instructors still must judge technique and expression, adapt in real time, manage groups and minors, demonstrate physical or musical skills, and remain accountable for educational quality. Implementation will depend on integrated school-management workflows and disciplined human review.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed recurring tuition allows operators to retain initial labor savings, especially when avoided hiring does not visibly reduce service. Retention erodes as local competitors adopt similar tools, instructors bargain for part of productivity gains, or parents expect lower prices, smaller classes, or better programming. Schools that convert savings into greater instructor consistency and customer responsiveness may preserve more value than those that simply reduce staff.
narrative.firm_availability: The LMM population is limited by the supplied estimate of 74 firms. Broad owner surveys show meaningful five-year sale or transfer intentions, and marketplace data confirm completed studio transactions, but many arts schools are bound to the founder's reputation or teaching role. Multi-instructor schools with documented curriculum, recurring billing, assignable leases, stable enrollment, and distributed parent relationships are more transferable than personality-led practices.
narrative.demand_durability: Self-enrichment teaching employment is projected to grow modestly over a decade, consistent with near-flat to slightly positive five-year real demand. Fine-arts instruction benefits from sustained interest in creative development, enrichment, and community performance, but is discretionary and sensitive to household budgets, youth demographics, competing activities, and free digital instruction. Live human-led quantity should remain dominant, with hybrid delivery taking a larger role.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The occupation evidence is four-digit rather than 611610-specific, current AI-use evidence comes from public K-12 teachers, and no public dataset directly measures task exposure, automation pass-through, or AI-only substitution in private arts schools. The NAICS code spans disciplines with different facility and instructor needs. Founder dependence can simultaneously reduce eligibility, completed transfers, and post-transfer customer retention; diligence must avoid counting that same weakness as a favorable labor opportunity.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring tuition plus automatable administration and preparation can convert workflow adoption into avoided hiring while preserving instructor-led delivery.
narrative.principal_weakness: The small LMM target pool and founder- or instructor-dependent customer relationships limit transferable firm availability.

sources.title: Other Schools and Instruction - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: The broader NAICS 611600 occupation mix and wages: educational instruction occupations were 51.97% of employment, self-enrichment teachers 30.23%, arts/media occupations 13.30%, office support 9.87%, and management 6.89%.

sources.title: ChatGPT and other AI tools are changing the teaching profession
sources.supports: A Gallup/Walton poll of more than 2,000 US public K-12 teachers found six in ten used AI; weekly users estimated about six hours saved, while the article documents lesson, assessment, administrative, privacy, and human-judgment constraints, including an art-teacher example.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Gallup reports 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; it also documents owner-labor dependence as a transfer barrier.

sources.title: Dance & Fitness Studio Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks
sources.supports: Reported completed dance and fitness studio transactions from 2021-2025, including annual median sale prices and sold-business earnings and revenue multiples, demonstrate an arm's-length transfer market for a partial industry proxy.

sources.title: Occupational projections and worker characteristics
sources.supports: BLS projects self-enrichment-teacher employment from 417,500 in 2024 to 433,000 in 2034, a 3.7% increase, and reports 16.8% self-employed in 2024.

sources.title: Arts Participation in 2022: A Technical Summary Report
sources.supports: The NEA identifies the 2022 Survey of Public Participation in the Arts as the nation's most comprehensive measure of adult arts engagement and includes analysis of learning in the arts by age, education, region, and metro status.
